description: The provided image appears to be a placeholder icon rather than the actual figure content. Based on the accompanying text extraction summary for the paper investigating Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels, this figure likely presents aging hardening curves or comprehensive mechanical property data comparing three alloy compositions: Fe-35Mn-15Al-5Cu, Fe-35Mn-17Al-5Cu, and Fe-35Mn-19Al-5Cu. The figure would display Vickers hardness (HV) values ranging from approximately 250 HV in the solution-treated state to peak values of 924-965 HV after aging at various temperatures (525-650°C) and times (1-10 hours), illustrating the effect of aluminum content on precipitation hardening kinetics. It may include multiple curves showing hardness evolution versus aging time at different temperatures, or comparative bar charts indicating maximum specific hardness values reaching 141 HV·cm³/g. Additionally, the figure might incorporate secondary microstructural data such as phase fractions (α, β-Mn, FCC Cu-rich, and BCC phases) or fracture toughness values (3.5-8.6 MPa·m^1/2) obtained from nanoindentation and indentation crack measurements. Without the actual visual content, specific graphical elements, axis scales, data markers, and curve trends cannot be directly verified.

Figure 1 presents a schematic diagram illustrating the Vickers indentation fracture toughness measurement methodology employed for evaluating the mechanical properties of aged Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels. The image depicts a diamond-shaped indentation imprint with four radial cracks propagating from the corners, where $l_i$ denotes the radical crack length extending from each indentation corner. According to the caption and associated text, the average crack length $c$ is calculated as $0.25\sum_{i=1}^{4}l_i$, serving as a critical parameter in the fracture toughness calculation. The schematic demonstrates how an applied indentation load of 9.8 N generates these radial cracks in the peak-aged alloys (15Al, 17Al, and 19Al variants), enabling determination of fracture toughness ($K_{IC}$) using a formula incorporating elastic modulus (164–181 GPa), Vickers hardness, and crack length. This figure establishes the experimental basis for the reported fracture toughness values of 8.6, 6.6, and 3.5 MPa·m$^{1/2}$ for the 15Al, 17Al, and 19Al peak-aged samples respectively, supporting the conclusion that increasing Al content degrades fracture toughness despite enhancing hardness.

The figure presents the aging hardening kinetics of Fe-35Mn-xAl-5Cu (x = 15, 17, 19 at.%) low-density steels, displaying Vickers hardness evolution curves (left column) and corresponding 2D contour maps (right column) as functions of aging temperature (525–650°C) and time (0–10 h). Rows (a), (b), and (c) correspond to the 15Al, 17Al, and 19Al alloys, respectively. The line graphs track hardness development at six specific aging temperatures, while the contour plots color-code hardness values from 250 HV (blue/black) to 1000 HV (red/gray). The 15Al alloy exhibits rapid hardening at lower temperatures (525–575°C), reaching peak hardness (~916–965 HV) within 3–6 hours. Conversely, the 17Al and 19Al alloys show significantly retarded kinetics at 525°C, with 19Al showing minimal hardening; their peak hardness (~918–925 HV) requires higher temperatures (575–600°C) and longer times. The contour plots visually confirm that the optimal high-hardness window (>900 HV, red zones) systematically shifts toward higher aging temperatures as Al content increases, demonstrating that achieving ultra-high specific hardness requires elevated aging temperatures in higher-Al compositions.

Electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) inverse pole figure maps and corresponding phase distribution maps for solution-treated Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels with varying aluminum contents: (a) 15Al (Fe-35Mn-15Al-5Cu), (b) 17Al (Fe-35Mn-17Al-5Cu), and (c) 19Al (Fe-35Mn-19Al-5Cu). The inverse pole figures display crystallographic orientations using the standard [001]-[101]-[111] color key, revealing equiaxed grain structures with scale bars of 500 μm (a, c) and 200 μm (b). The accompanying phase maps indicate that all three alloys in the solution-treated condition (1100°C/10 min/oil quench) consist entirely of the α phase (body-centered cubic, BCC), shown in red, with no γ phase (face-centered cubic, FCC) present. This confirms the single-phase BCC microstructure prior to aging treatments. The figure establishes the baseline microstructural state, demonstrating that increasing Al content from 15 to 19 at.% maintains the single α-phase equilibrium in the solution-treated state, providing the foundation for subsequent β-Mn precipitation during aging.

This figure presents EBSD inverse pole figure (IPF) maps and corresponding phase distribution maps of the 15Al Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steel subjected to different aging treatments. The left panels (a1-c1) show low-magnification overviews with scale bars of 100-200 μm, while the right panels (a2-c2) display magnified views at 5 μm scale. Subfigures (a1-a2) reveal the microstructure after aging at 525°C for 3 h, consisting predominantly of β-Mn phase (green in phase map, 85.8% by area) with residual untransformed α matrix (BCC, red). After aging at 550°C for 6 h (b1-b2), the α matrix has almost entirely transformed into β-Mn, and a new fine BCC phase is present within the β-Mn. Following aging at 650°C for 3 h (c1-c2), no untransformed α regions remain, and a new FCC phase (magenta) is distributed within β-Mn. The color legend identifies BCC (red), α-Mn (blue), β-Mn (green), and FCC (magenta), with IPF triangle legends indicating crystallographic orientations. These microstructural observations confirm that β-Mn precipitation drives rapid aging hardening (hardness increasing from 253.4 HV to 872.0 HV), while secondary BCC and FCC phases form at higher temperatures.

Figure 5 displays EBSD inverse pole figure (IPF) maps and phase distribution maps for the Fe-35Mn-17Al-5Cu (17Al) low-density steel alloy subjected to different aging treatments. Panels a1 and a2 show the microstructure after aging at 525°C for 10 h, where only 20.5% β-Mn (green) has precipitated along α grain boundaries and within grains, alongside small α-Mn particles (blue). Panels b1 and b2 present the sample aged at 575°C for 6 h, revealing nearly complete transformation of the α matrix to β-Mn with fine new FCC phase precipitates (pink) distributed within the β-Mn. Panels c1 and c2 illustrate the microstructure after aging at 650°C for 10 h, showing complete α-to-β-Mn transformation accompanied by new BCC phase particles (red) within the β-Mn matrix. The magnified views (a2-c2) clearly resolve these secondary phases at interfaces. These micrographs demonstrate that higher Al content slows β-Mn precipitation kinetics compared to the 15Al alloy, while elevated aging temperatures promote the formation of secondary BCC and FCC phases that affect the hardening response.

This figure presents EBSD inverse pole figure (IPF) maps and corresponding phase maps of Fe-35Mn-19Al-5Cu (19Al) low-density steel aged at different temperatures, specifically (a) 525°C for 10 h, (b) 600°C for 3 h, and (c) 650°C for 6 h, with (a₂-c₂) showing magnified views. The IPF maps display crystallographic orientations while phase maps identify four distinct phases: BCC (α-matrix, red), α-Mn (blue), β-Mn (green), and FCC (magenta). At 525°C/10 h (a₁, a₂), the microstructure consists primarily of untransformed α-grains with only 12.1% β-Mn precipitating along grain boundaries as dendritic structures, plus minor α-Mn particles, consistent with slowed precipitation kinetics in high-Al alloys. After aging at 600°C/3 h (b₁, b₂), the matrix has almost completely transformed to β-Mn (green), with small amounts of new FCC phase (magenta) visible at β-Mn interfaces. At 650°C/6 h (c₁, c₂), the α-matrix is fully consumed by β-Mn, and notably, a great amount of new BCC phase (red) precipitates within the β-Mn grains. The scale bars range from 500 μm for overview images to 5-10 μm for magnified views. These observations demonstrate that increasing Al content significantly retards β-Mn precipitation at lower temperatures, while high-temperature aging promotes complete α→β-Mn transformation accompanied by secondary phase formation (FCC and BCC) at β-Mn interfaces or interiors, which affects the aging hardening behavior and fracture toughness.

This figure presents secondary electron (SE) images showing the microstructural evolution of Fe-35Mn-15Al-5Cu (15Al) low-density steel during isothermal aging at temperatures ranging from 525°C to 650°C for various durations (1–10 h). The 16-panel montage captures the progressive phase transformation from the α matrix to β-Mn precipitates, with insets providing higher-magnification views (scale bars of 5 or 10 μm) revealing detailed precipitate morphologies. At 525°C/1 h, fine α-Mn particles are dispersed within the α matrix. By 525°C/3 h, short rod-shaped β-Mn precipitates appear along grain boundaries and within grains, coexisting with untransformed α. After 6 h at 525°C, the microstructure becomes predominantly β-Mn. At higher temperatures (550–600°C) or extended times (10 h), new secondary precipitates form at β-Mn interfaces. The images demonstrate that complete transformation to β-Mn—critical for achieving ultrahigh specific hardness—occurs rapidly within 3 h at 525°C for the 15Al alloy, whereas higher Al contents retard this transformation.

This figure presents secondary electron SEM micrographs showing the microstructural evolution of Fe-35Mn-17Al-5Cu (17Al) low-density steel subjected to aging treatments at temperatures ranging from 525°C to 650°C for durations of 1–10 hours. The images capture the progressive precipitation and growth of β-Mn phase (labeled in green) from the BCC α-matrix. At 525°C, β-Mn initially forms along grain boundaries and grows into short rod-shaped particles within grains over time (1h → 10h). At 550°C, similar evolution occurs with an inset at 1h revealing α-Mn particles. At 575°C, the microstructure shows nearly complete transformation to β-Mn, with insets at 6h and 10h indicating the appearance of new fine precipitates. Higher temperature aging at 600°C, 625°C, and 650°C displays extensive β-Mn formation with additional new precipitates (labeled in magenta) distributed at interfaces. All micrographs include 50 μm scale bars, with selected higher-magnification insets (2 μm or 10 μm scale bars) revealing fine precipitate details. The series demonstrates that increasing aging temperature accelerates β-Mn transformation but promotes secondary phase formation, while the caption and context indicate these microstructural changes correlate with aging hardening behavior and fracture toughness evolution in the alloy system.

This figure presents a comprehensive set of secondary electron (SE) images captured via scanning electron microscopy, illustrating the microstructural evolution of Fe-35Mn-19Al-5Cu (19Al) low-density steel subjected to systematic aging treatments. The 16 sub-images document the temporal and thermal progression of phase transformations across temperatures ranging from 525°C to 650°C with holding times of 1, 3, 6, and 10 hours. At lower temperatures (525°C), fine α-Mn particles initially precipitate within the α-matrix, gradually transforming into β-Mn phase with extended time (6-10 h). As aging temperature increases to 550-575°C, the microstructure reveals distinct β-Mn precipitation along grain boundaries and within grains, with residual "Untransformed α" regions visible at intermediate stages. At higher temperatures (600-650°C), the images capture the emergence of "New precipitates" (identified in accompanying text as FCC Cu-rich and BCC phases) distributed at β-Mn interfaces. The scale bars (predominantly 50 μm, with 10 μm insets for high-magnification detail) enable quantitative assessment of phase morphology and distribution. These micrographs provide direct visual evidence that increasing Al content retards β-Mn precipitation kinetics at lower temperatures, while promoting secondary phase formation at higher temperatures, thereby elucidating the microstructural basis for the observed aging hardening behavior and peak hardness optimization in ultra-high specific hardness steels.

X-ray diffraction patterns of Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels comparing phase evolution across three aluminum compositions subjected to specific aging treatments selected based on prior microstructural analysis: 15Al alloy aged at 550°C for 1h (black curve), 17Al alloy aged at 575°C for 1h (red curve), and 19Al alloy aged at 550°C for 6h (blue curve). The patterns display diffraction intensity versus 2θ angle ranging from 20° to 110°, with crystallographic peaks indexed and phase identifications marked for α/D0₃ (diamond symbol), α-Mn (club symbol), and β-Mn (spade symbol). Visible diffraction peaks include (110), (210), (400), (221), (411), (310), (332), (422), (510), (321), (410), (330), (420), (444), (721), (520), and (521) planes. The patterns clearly show the coexistence of both α-Mn and β-Mn diffraction peaks in all three alloys, indicating coprecipitation of these phases during aging before the α matrix undergoes complete transformation to β-Mn. Variations in relative peak intensities among the three compositions reflect the influence of Al content (15–19 at.%) and aging parameters on precipitation kinetics, supporting the conclusion that increased Al content slows β-Mn formation while altering phase fraction distributions.

This figure presents comprehensive transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization of the Fe-35Mn-15Al-5Cu (15Al) low-density steel aged at 600°C for 6 h. Panel (a) shows low-magnification bright-field (BF) and corresponding dark-field (DF) images revealing complete disappearance of the α matrix and formation of abundant near-equiaxed β-Mn grains. Panel (b) displays higher magnification BF imaging of β-Mn particles alongside selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns from circled regions: the green circle confirms the β-Mn matrix structure with [012] beam axis, while orange and cyan circles from fine particles at interfaces show FCC crystalline structure with [011] beam axis and visible FCC twins. Panel (c) presents a BF image with corresponding EDS elemental mapping (Fe, Mn, Al, Cu), demonstrating that the interfacial FCC phase is rich in Cu (magenta) while depleted in Fe and Mn, whereas β-Mn is poor in Cu. These results elucidate the microstructural origin of ultra-high specific hardness through complete β-Mn transformation and Cu-rich FCC precipitate formation.

Figure 12 presents comprehensive TEM characterization of the Fe-35Mn-17Al-5Cu (17Al) alloy aged at 575°C for 6 hours. Panel (a) shows low-magnification bright-field (BF) images revealing the distribution of precipitates within the matrix. Panel (b) provides high-magnification BF imaging together with EDS elemental mapping (Fe, Mn, Al, Cu), demonstrating the elemental distribution between phases, and a selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern from the highlighted region confirming FCC twins in the Cu-rich phase. Panel (c) displays BF images of β-Mn precipitates with corresponding SAED pattern along the [111] beam direction, indexing the β-Mn crystal structure, alongside high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images showing the atomic lattice. Panel (d) presents HRTEM imaging of the interface between the FCC Cu-rich phase and β-Mn, with Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) patterns from both regions confirming their crystallographic structures. The figure reveals the coexistence of hard β-Mn phase and FCC Cu-rich precipitates containing twins, supporting the mechanism of Cu rejection from β-Mn during aging and the formation of secondary phases at interfaces.

This figure presents nanoindentation results for Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels with varying Al contents (15Al, 17Al, and 19Al) under optimized aging conditions. Panel (a) displays nanoindentation load-displacement curves showing the 15Al alloy aged at 600°C for 6 h exhibits an average hardness (H_Ave) of 11.7 ± 0.4 GPa, the 17Al alloy aged at 575°C for 6 h shows 11.1 ± 0.6 GPa, and the 19Al alloy aged at 600°C for 10 h shows 10.4 ± 1.3 GPa. The error bars and curve spreads indicate increasing data scatter with higher Al content. Panels (b) show secondary electron (SE) images of the indentation arrays with corresponding hardness values (in GPa) labeled for each indent. The 15Al sample shows relatively uniform hardness values ranging from 10.9 to 12.4 GPa, while the 19Al sample exhibits greater variation (8.0 to 12.9 GPa), visually confirming the increased standard deviation. According to the caption and text, these measurements were taken from areas avoiding FCC Cu-rich and BCC phases to determine the intrinsic hardness of β-Mn precipitates. The results demonstrate that although increasing Al content slightly enhances the intrinsic hardness of β-Mn through solid-solution strengthening (from 12.1 GPa in 15Al to 12.5 GPa in 19Al), the overall micro-hardness decreases due to the increased volume fraction of softer FCC Cu-rich and BCC phases at β-Mn interfaces.

This bar chart presents SEM-EDS quantitative analysis results showing the atomic percentages of Mn, Al, and Cu in the β-Mn phase for three Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steel alloys (15Al, 17Al, and 19Al) in their peak-aged (hardest) conditions. The x-axis displays the three measured elements, while grouped bars represent each alloy composition. The data reveals that Mn content remains relatively constant across all three alloys at approximately 36.2–36.8 at.%, and Cu content stays near 2.6–2.8 at.%. In contrast, Al content in the β-Mn phase increases systematically with nominal alloy composition, rising from 14.3 at.% in 15Al to 16.3 at.% in 17Al and 17.5 at.% in 19Al. These results demonstrate that higher Al additions lead to increased Al dissolution into the β-Mn phase. The caption states this solid-solution strengthening slightly enhances the intrinsic hardness of β-Mn (from 12.1 to 12.5 GPa), though the overall alloy hardness decreases with increasing Al content due to simultaneous formation of softer FCC Cu-rich and BCC phases at β-Mn interfaces.

This figure displays correlated EBSD and EDS characterization results for Fe-35Mn-xAl-5Cu (x=15, 17, 19 at.%) low-density steels aged at 525°C, comprising forescatter detector (FSD) images, inverse pole figures (IPF), phase maps, and elemental mapping of Fe, Mn, Al, and Cu. The three rows correspond to 15Al (3 h), 17Al (10 h), and 19Al (10 h) aging conditions. FSD images reveal distinct microstructural morphologies evolving from fine cellular precipitates in 15Al to coarse blocky structures in 17Al and elongated features in 19Al. Phase maps identify β-Mn, BCC, α-Mn, and FCC phases with varying fractions, showing progressively incomplete α-to-β-Mn transformation with increasing Al content despite extended aging time. EDS mapping confirms that β-Mn precipitates are enriched in Mn while depleted in Al and Cu, supporting the mechanism that higher Al content hinders β-Mn precipitation kinetics by requiring extensive Al rejection during nucleation and growth.

This figure presents an Ashby-style materials selection map plotting specific hardness (hardness/density, HV·cm³/g) against density (g/cm³) to compare Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels against existing material classes. The scatter plot displays data clusters for three alloy variants (15Al, 17Al, and 19Al, representing Fe-35Mn-xAl-5Cu compositions) shown as colored point groups in the low-density region (~6.6–7.0 g/cm³), achieving ultra-high specific hardness values exceeding 140 HV·cm³/g. These are contrasted with other low-density steels (orange circles), conventional steels including stainless and high-speed steels (pink squares), pure iron (black star at ~7.8 g/cm³), and medium/high entropy alloys M/HEAs (blue hexagons), which predominantly occupy higher density regions (>7.5 g/cm³) with specific hardness below 120 HV·cm³/g (indicated by a dashed reference line). The 15Al alloy demonstrates the optimal performance, reaching specific hardness of ~140 HV·cm³/g while maintaining fracture toughness of 8.6 MPa·m^1/2. The figure establishes that the developed Fe-Mn-Al-Cu steels significantly outperform existing steels and M/HEAs, breaking through the typical ~120 HV·cm³/g specific hardness limit while maintaining low density.
